US President Trump says since last month more than 100 million barrels of oil have left the Persian Gulf in a “secret” US mission to get tankers out via the Strait of Hormuz. 

If true, and counting 40 days (all May and first 9 days of June) that would equate to ~2.5m b/d. https://t.co/Lo5IJvmPco
U.S./IRAN try to maximise leverage in negotiations while avoiding uncontrolled escalation: https://t.co/J9qzmflbvw

Oil Prices Slide as Traders Prepare for Blockade to End

Crude oil prices are falling, with the sharpest declines in the spot market and near-dated futures contracts, even as global petroleum inventories deplete rapidly to the lowest for the time of year since 2008.

The entire forward pricing curve has flattened significantly since the end of April after steepening in the first two months of the war between the United States and Iran.

The speed and scale of the depletion would ordinarily have resulted in a much bigger rise in spot and near-dated futures contracts and a fiercer backwardation.

But traders have become increasingly convinced tanker traffic through the Strait will resume soon, either as a result of a diplomatic agreement or increased military protection of vessels by the United States ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

text:
This whole talk about dark transit is really silly. Serious barrel counters will have already included 2 million b/d of dark transit like we have for Iran. The blockade doesn’t work. 

But the discussion misses an even more important point and that’s the production shut in math doesn’t change. Unless you have a steady flow of empty tankers going back in with visibility for MORE empty tankers, you don’t bring back shut in production.

We probably would have seen more folks risking the dark transits through Hormuz earlier if Trump didn’t keep saying it would reopen next week—for three months.

Taking the bigger risk feels like something you do when you you’re less confident a safer option is arriving anytime soon
"We don't need anything that Canada has" https://t.co/gQCuObBHAw

Chinese refinery runs has hit rock bottom. 

Beijing just dumped a 13mt product export quota onto the tape, but with policymakers heavily defending domestic supply and margins completely in the gutter, any actual increase in exports is bound to be highly restricted. 

It’s a massive win for the rest of the Asian refining complex, but the pain for Chinese refiners isn't clearing up anytime soon.

Highlights from EIAs weekly report:
Crude stocks fell for a seventh straight week by 7.2mb to a 2022 seasonal low. 
Cushing fell to 21.6 mb, near but still above operational minimum levels around 20 mb
SPR inventories fell 7.9 mb, holding just above the 40-year low from 2023
Production saw a meaningful increase of 92 kb/d to 13.8 mb/d  
Crude export slowed 1mb/d to 4.8 mb/d, with total crude and fuel exports falling to nine-week low at 12.5 mb/d.
Refinery utilisation held at a 2018 high just over 95%, with jet fuel production hitting a fresh record
Diesel stockpiles slumped to a 1990 seasonal low
Implied demand for gasoline (4-wk avg.) held steady while distillate rose 109 kb/d

US CPI came in broadly in line with expectations with the negative supply chock lifting headline inflation to 4.2%, its highest level in three years, while core inflation edged up to 2.9%. The figures underpins the market's current focus on persistent inflation risks, driven by higher energy prices, and the prospect of higher-for-longer interest rates. 
On a 3-month annualised based the headline #inflation reached 8%.

Here are the latest stock market updates. Markets remain on edge as tech volatility and Middle East tensions overshadow an in‑line inflation report.

Market Wrap: Tech Drags Indexes Lower

Wall Street extended declines on Wednesday as technology stocks remained under pressure and renewed US‑Iran tensions overshadowed a tame inflation reading. 

The Nasdaq Composite lost 1.18% to 25,375, while the S&P 500 slipped 0.74% to 7,349 (unofficial). The Dow Jones Industrial Average, buoyed by a defensive rotation, managed a 0.20% gain to close around 50,877.

European and Asian markets also felt the weight: 
• Germany's DAX lost 0.97% and 

• France's CAC 40 fell 0.51%, while 

• Japan's Nikkei fell more than 2% amid a global flight from risk assets.

Key Drivers

Geopolitics: US forces launched self‑defense strikes against Iran at President Trump's direction, after Iran shot down a US Apache helicopter over the Strait of Hormuz. 

The strait remains partially blocked, keeping ~20% of global oil supply at risk.

Fed outlook: Goldman Sachs has abandoned its forecast for a Fed rate cut in December 2026, now expecting two quarter‑point cuts only in June and December 2027.

Individual Stock Movers

Super Micro Computer ($SMCI ) fell as much as 19% after unveiling a $7 billion equity raise to fund AI server components, raising dilution concerns after the stock had gained 39% YTD.

Lakeland Industries ($LAKE ) rose 15% on earnings. Cracker Barrel (CBRL) added 8%. Fuller Smith & Turner jumped 8.5% after a top‑and‑bottom‑line beat.

Nvidia ($NVDA ) fell over 2%, extending a 5‑day chip sell‑off that erased $1.4 trillion from semiconductor market caps.

IPO Market Heats Up

SpaceX saw demand four times its target raise, attracting over $250 billion in investor interest for its Friday Nasdaq debut.

OpenAI confidentially filed for a US IPO, joining rival Anthropic in pushing toward public markets to tap surging AI investor demand.

The report I published yesterday, when fear was dominant again and everything felt dark. Enjoy the read 👇
June 10, 2026 US Consumer Price Index CPI Report

MarketMaestro

21 hours ago
The market is locked onto Consumer Price Index, CPI, data. This data set is seen as the most critical macroeconomic crossroads that will determine market direction on the eve of the first Federal Open Market Committee, FOMC, meeting under new Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh, amid the energy shocks created by escalating geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East, new tariff walls in supply chains, and unprecedented structural cost increases created by artificial intelligence investments.

General Consensus and Expectation Ranges

According to analyst estimates collected by FactSet, headline CPI is expected to rise 0.5% month over month in May and climb from the previous month’s 3.8% level to 4.2% year over year. Core CPI, which excludes volatile items such as food and energy and is the main focus of monetary policy, is expected to rise 0.3% month over month and reach 2.9% year over year.

Goldman Sachs: The institution, which has largely abandoned rate cut expectations because of labor market resilience, expects Core Personal Consumption Expenditures, PCE, inflation to gradually fall to 2.1% by December.

JPMorgan Chase: The institution’s Chief US Economist Michael Feroli draws attention to the fragile impact of the May CPI data on market psychology. According to Feroli, an upside inflation surprise above expectations would completely shatter the inflation is transitory narrative in markets and pull forward the timing of the first rate hike. JPMorgan’s models show that in such an inflationary scenario, the probability of the Fed hiking rates by September 2026 would exceed 50%, and that a second hike would start to be priced by December. I have seen this institution be wrong many times.

Morgan Stanley: Matt Hornbach says the May CPI data to be released will be spicier, and that the pressure created by high energy and AI infrastructure costs will keep inflation alive. On the other hand, Chief Global Economist Seth Carpenter expects global growth to be 3.2% in 2026 and inflationary pressures to soften as oil prices decline toward $90 by year end.

Deutsche Bank: The bank offers one of the most aggressive headline inflation estimates on Wall Street, 4.29%, based on its projection of a sharp 6.8% monthly increase in gasoline prices specifically for CPI.

Citi and Barclays: Citi analysts confirm that headline inflation will jump to 4.2%, while saying the real key question is whether there will be a month over month slowdown in core CPI.

Real time market data, platforms such as Zillow, and independent rent indexes show that new rent increases across the US have largely stopped, and that real rent costs are easing as housing supply increases and landlords activate aggressive incentives such as free months to find tenants. However, the survey based OER calculation method used by the BLS follows market realities with a 6 to 12 month lag. As Jim Bianco from Bianco Research also noted, a statistical anomaly caused by the government shutdown threat last September continues to artificially inflate shelter figures. This methodological lag will continue to cause shelter inflation to remain high on paper in the May CPI report, despite cooling in the real economy.

For the May CPI to be released in June, the T-1 signal in Truflation systems produced warnings before the official print, indicating that live market pricing in key components points to upward pressure and that the official print is likely to surprise. However, Truflation Chief Data Officer Oliver Rust says: High figures from the BLS are entirely due to instant jumps in energy and methodological statistical errors in shelter; despite the strong surface appearance in the labor market, the underlying core economy is actually softening rapidly.

Kevin Warsh will chair the first FOMC meeting of his career on June 16-17.

Warsh is expected to completely remove the rate cut friendly bias toward easing from the official statement and shift to a neutral stance policy position. The new Fed Chair is expected to adopt a strategy of keeping rates high for longer by citing stubborn CPI data, in order not to repeat the past inflation is transitory mistake.

Market Scenarios After the Data

If headline inflation approaches Deutsche Bank’s extreme estimates and pushes toward 4.3%, and especially if core goods, because of tariffs, and shelter, because of lagged OER effects, surprise to the upside, the initial market reaction could be extremely destructive.

If the data comes exactly in line with the FactSet median and Bank of America estimates, markets will quickly calm down after the data volatility and shift focus to the FOMC meeting one week later and Kevin Warsh’s communication.

If headline data falls below 4.0% and core inflation records an increase lower than 0.2%, this could create excitement in markets. But this probability currently looks difficult.

The Cleveland Fed’s dynamic model expects headline inflation at 4.05%, below consensus, and projects a more stable path for core inflation at 0.23% month over month and 2.83% year over year. On the other hand, Truflation’s real time data fell to a low level of 1.83%. They see OER and shelter items in BLS data as structural noise.

🦇Batman Rational Forecast for May 2026 CPI

• Headline CPI, monthly: 0.38% to 0.42% range. Direct reflections of the geopolitical energy shock will be effective, but consensus seems overly pessimistic.

• Headline CPI, yearly: 4.0% to 4.1% range. Slightly below the market consensus of 4.2%, closer to the Cleveland Fed’s 4.05% expectation.

• Core CPI, monthly: 0.20% to 0.25% range.

• Core CPI, yearly: flat around 2.8%.

Ultimately, although the data that will hit screens on the morning of June 10 will look uncomfortable at the headline level because of high energy costs and lagged shelter effects, when looking into core details, it may carry signs of structural cooling. The new Federal Reserve leadership under Kevin Warsh will have to take this geopolitical noise in headline figures into account while building its communication strategy, but in the background, it will be aware that restrictive monetary policy is creating a disinflationary gravitational pull in real consumer demand.

In this scenario, if the market chooses tomorrow to strip out the energy and shelter noise in the headline and buy the cooling in the core, we may see a controlled upward move. However, a print at or above the consensus boundary could send hedging costs in the market flying ahead of Kevin Warsh. Because since this is the new Fed Chair’s first meeting, nobody wants to take risk in this uncertainty.

quotedTweet.text: Inflation came in exactly as I expected!

text: https://t.co/N8QntYWchk

CPI DAY IS HERE🚨

The May CPI report will be released today at 8:30 AM ET.

Here's what you need to know:

- Wall Street expects headline CPI to come in around 4.2% YoY, up from 3.8% previously.

- The increase is largely being driven by higher energy prices following the escalation in the Middle East and the renewed oil ( $WTI $CL $BRENT) shock. Core CPI is expected to remain much softer.

- The median forecast from major banks is 0.22% MoM and 2.8% YoY, suggesting underlying inflation pressures may still be relatively contained despite rising headline numbers.

Forecasts vary significantly:

- JP Morgan expects 0.27% Core CPI
- Goldman Sachs expects 0.17%
- Bank of America expects 0.20%
- Barclays expects 0.28%

The market reaction will likely depend more on Core CPI than the headline figure.

According to JP Morgan's trading desk, a Core CPI reading above 0.35% could send the S&P 500 ( $SPX) down 2% to 3%:

- 0.20%-0.25% Core CPI: Stocks could gain up to 1%
- Below 0.20% Core CPI: Relief rally potential of 1.5%-2%.

Oil remains the wild card. Several inflation models show the recent Hormuz-related oil spike pushing inflation higher in the coming months if energy prices remain elevated.

The last five CPI releases all produced volatility across equities, rates, and crypto, even when the data came in close to expectations. The timing couldn't be more important.

Markets are already dealing with one of the most volatile weeks of the year:

- The U.S. and Iran are openly exchanging strikes
- Asian markets are under pressure
- Tech stocks just experienced one of their largest intraday drawdowns in years.
- Crypto remains in fear territory

This means today's CPI report has the potential to either calm markets or add fuel to the current sell-off.

A hot inflation print could strengthen the case for higher-for-longer rates and put additional pressure on stocks ( $NVDA $TSLA $MU) and crypto ( $BTC $ETH $SOL $HYPE).

A cooler-than-expected print could give us the relief rally we have been waiting for.

quotedTweet.text:
GM

A LOT HAPPENED OVERNIGHT:

1. The biggest story remains the escalating conflict between the U.S. and Iran. After a U.S. Apache helicopter was reportedly shot down, American forces launched multiple waves of strikes targeting Iranian military infrastructure. Iran responded with threats and claimed retaliatory drone attacks against U.S. military assets across the region. Markets are now watching whether the conflict expands further.

2. Israel also continued strikes against Hezbollah-linked infrastructure in southern Lebanon. Ceasefire has failed again.

3. The Nasdaq ( $NQ $QQQ)experienced one of its largest intraday drawdowns in years, falling as much as 5.3% before buyers stepped in aggressively. Despite the panic, buyers managed to push the index well off its lows into the close, showing that dip-buying activity remains strong.

4. Asian markets are under pressure again this morning. Taiwan's equity market fell more than 2% as investors reacted to rising geopolitical risks and weakness across global technology stocks.

5. Anthropic launched Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5, its latest generation of AI models. The release adds another major development to the AI race that continues to dominate market narratives.

6. Crypto markets remain under pressure. Total crypto market capitalization sits near $2.11T, down 2.1% over the last 24 hours. Fear & Greed remains deeply in Fear territory at 14/100.

7. Among the majors, $BTC is down 2.3% to $61.4K, $ETH is down 2.4% to $1,629, $BNB is down 1.8%, $SOL is down 2.4%, and $HYPE is one of the larger losers among major assets, falling more than 8% over the last 24 hours.

8. Despite the broader weakness, some tokens managed to outperform. WBT led with a gain of 13.7%, followed by BEAT (+15.5%), HASH (+9.8%), STABLE (+9.8%), and MORPHO (+8.8%). On the downside, SIREN collapsed 35.1%, LAB fell 26.6%, DEXE lost 11.2%, HYPE dropped 8.1%, and VVV declined 6.7%.

9. Investor sentiment continues to deteriorate. Nearly 44% of Americans now say they are financially worse off than a year ago, the highest reading since early 2023 and another sign that economic concerns remain elevated despite resilient markets.

10. Today's main event is CPI. Markets are already dealing with war headlines, extreme volatility in tech stocks, weakness across crypto, and growing macro uncertainty. If inflation comes in hotter than expected, it could add another layer of pressure and extend the current sell-off across risk assets.

THE NEXT 24 HOURS COULD SET THE TONE FOR THE REST OF THE WEEK.

text:
J.P. Morgan's Nifty 2026 Outlook: The Full Picture - 

Several reports are highlighting J.P. Morgan's 20500 Nifty target, creating unnecessary #panic. But here's what the report actually says -

Revised Nifty 50 Targets ( Year End 2026 )

Bull Case: 30000 ( Earlier: 33000 )

Base Case: 27000 ( Earlier: 30000 )

Bear Case: 20500 ( Earlier: 24000 )

The widely discussed 20500 target is not J.P. Morgan's primary forecast. It represents a #worst case scenario driven by -

A prolonged oil price shock

Disruptions in the Strait of #Hormuz

Earnings downgrade across sectors

Weakening market sentiment

Why did J.P. Morgan turn #cautious ?

Indian equities continue to trade at a premium to Emerging Markets.

Earnings growth expectations have been lowered.

Higher crude oil prices could hurt margins and consumption.

Limited #AI and #semiconductor exposure in Indian large caps.

Heavy supply from #IPOs, QIPs, and #promoter stake sales.

Bottom Line - 

J.P. Morgan's base case remains 27000, while 20500 is merely a downside risk scenario. Despite near-term concerns, the firm continues to believe in India's long-term structural growth story.

Bear Case ≠ Base Case.

#Nifty50 #IndianMarkets #StockMarket #Investing #JPMorgan

Daily Digest-6/9

📷DOW NASDAQ S&P 500 RUSSELL 2000

US stocks ended mixed as renewed tech and chip weakness and muted oil moves under geopolitical strain set cautious tone ahead of CPI data

S&P 500 (SPY) closed modestly lower, Nasdaq (QQQ) fell more sharply, and Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA) eked out a gain, reflecting rotation away from tech into industrials and defensives

Semiconductor stocks reversed an early rebound, with iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) and PHLX Semiconductor Index both declining as investors continued to move out of overheated AI-linked names
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) fell during the session and dropped further after-hours after announcing a $7 billion equity financing that overshadowed its strong AI server backlog
Marvell Technology (MRVL), Micron Technology (MU), Broadcom (AVGO), Apple (AAPL), Salesforce (CRM), AMD (AMD), and Cisco (CSCO) all weakened, contributing to pressure on the information technology sector
Dow strength was supported by gains in Home Depot (HD), Sherwin-Williams (SHW), Nike (NKE), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Visa (V), JPMorgan (JPM), and other industrials, financials, and consumer staples names
Nine of eleven S&P 500 (SPY) sectors advanced, led by financials, materials, and consumer staples, while information technology and energy lagged
Oil (@CL) prices were muted despite US-Iran tensions, reports of a downed US helicopter, and comments from Donald Trump on a potential response to Iran
US trade data showed a narrower April deficit, but the release had limited market impact amid ongoing inflation concerns
J.M. Smucker (SJM) rose after-hours after beating earnings estimates as investors looked ahead to Wednesday’s CPI report and additional earnings for direction

🔴 $QQQ $693.69 (-2.00%) (close)
Reports out of the White House indicate Trump is meeting in the Situation Room to discuss additional strikes on Iran, with two sources telling Axios he is weighing a large-scale but short-term military operation aimed at pressuring Tehran. Markets sold off hard, with SPY closing down 1.58%, QQQ off 2.00%, and WTI crude surging 3.06% to $90.90 on the geopolitical risk premium. VIX spiked 17.44%, signaling elevated fear ahead of Thursday's open.

🔴 $QQQ $693.69 (-2.00%) (close)
Iran's UN Ambassador publicly rejected negotiations under pressure, directly contradicting the optimistic tone from recent headlines suggesting a deal was close. This hardens the diplomatic standoff and likely reinforces the risk-off move already visible today, with WTI crude up 3.06% to $90.90, the VIX surging 17.44% to $22.22, and the S&P 500 down 1.58%.

📉 AryaFin Session Audit | Wednesday, June 10, 2026

Audit: A dual shockwave of escalating Middle East military friction and a persistent sell-off in hyper-scale artificial intelligence multiples triggered an aggressive institutional risk-off liquidation event, sending major benchmarks to deep session lows.  

📊 Major Benchmarks Core Settlement Matrix

Nasdaq Composite ($COMP:IND): 🔴 -2.0% down to 25,169.50 (Leading the broad liquidation as mega-cap tech valuations take the brunt of multiple compression).  

Dow Jones Industrial Average ($DJI): 🔴 -1.9% down to 49,918.78 (Sinking over 950 points as value layers buckle under systemic macro gravity).  

S&P 500 Index ($SP500): 🔴 -1.6% down to 7,266.99 (Marking its first back-to-back daily decline in over three weeks).  

Russell 2000 ($RUT): 🔴 -1.1% down to 2,835.46 (Small-cap equities exhibiting minor relative defense but closing firmly in the red).  

CBOE Volatility Index ($VIX): 🟢 Spiked to 20.84 (+4.88% as derivative desks aggressively bid up near-term hedging premiums).  

⚡ Systematic Catalysts & Market Movers

The Geopolitical Shockwave: Market sentiment fractured after the U.S. military executed what leadership termed "self-defense strikes" against targets in Iran, following the downing of a U.S. Army Apache helicopter near the Strait of Hormuz. 

The sudden friction severely rattled previous Middle East ceasefire expectations, sending WTI Crude Oil up +1.41% to settle at $89.46 per barrel on fears of localized energy infrastructure disruptions.  

The CPI Reality Check: The May Consumer Price Index (CPI) report printed hot, driven extensively by high sticky energy comps. While macro desks noted the core trend was slightly less severe than worst-case models anticipated, the baseline print reinforces that inflation forces remain too stubborn to permit near-term monetary relaxation by the Federal Reserve.  

AI Hardware Multiple Decompression: High-flying artificial intelligence pioneers continue to face intense technical valuation distribution. Institutional desks expressed growing concern that structural semiconductor valuations have shot up too far, too fast. 

Mega-caps suffered deep single-session erosions: Advanced Micro Devices ($AMD) dropped -3.02%, Apple ($AAPL) plummeted -3.64%, Microsoft ($MSFT) shed -2.02%, and Tesla ($TSLA) slid -3.00%.  

The Corporate Earnings Highlight: Bucking the systematic tech purge, Oracle Corporation ($ORCL) dropped a blockbusting Q4 report after the close, revealing a +21% surge in total quarterly revenue to $19.2B, heavily supercharged by +93% hyper-growth in its Cloud Infrastructure (IaaS) division.  

The Read: Enforcing Core Portfolio Defense. Today's aggressive distribution volume confirms that the equity market's historic 9-week positive run is undergoing a mandatory structural reset. 

When escalating international military friction cross-pollinates with climbing energy prices and a restrictive interest rate baseline, premium high-beta multiples simply cannot hold their ground. 

Managing risk exposure across intermediate portfolios remains vital until tech-sector liquidation flows fully dry up and an established accumulation shelf is carved out.  

#MacroAudit #WallStreet #TechRout #USIranStrikes #CPIData #OilSpike #AryaFin

RED ALERT: Iran War Is "Greatest Energy Threat in History" – IEA 🛢️🔥

The International Energy Agency (IEA) has officially issued its most dire warning to date: the 2026 Iran conflict represents the greatest global energy security threat in history. This isn't just another price hike—it is a total systemic fracture.  
The Brutal Reality:  
• Massive Supply Loss: The conflict has already cut off twice the volume of gas that Europe lost from Russia in 2022.  
• Chokepoint Crisis: With the Strait of Hormuz effectively blocked, 20% of global oil and LNG transit has ground to a halt.  
• Record Volatility: Brent Crude has surged over 47% since the start of the war, hovering near $110/barrel.  
• The Long Road Back: The IEA warns it could take at least six months just to restore basic operations at damaged oil and gas fields.  
From record prices at the pump to potential energy rationing in Europe and Asia, the "Great Energy Reset" is no longer a theory—it's here.  
#EnergyCrisis #IranWar2026 #OilPrices #IEA #BreakingNews #GlobalEconomy #StraitOfHormuz

Trump says the US has secretly been “taking millions of barrels of oil out of Iran every night.” The clip is going viral as proof America is stealing Iran’s crude. It is not, and the real story is quieter, true, and almost more interesting.

After US strikes knocked out Iranian radar, the Navy began quietly moving commercial tankers through the Strait of Hormuz at night with their transponders dark, getting them past Iranian fire. Around 100 million barrels have reached the market this way in a month. That helped cap the oil price, and it shows the US Navy can now push oil through a strait Iran spent months trying to shut.

But strip the bravado and three things collapse. The oil is not Iranian. Iran’s own exports have fallen under 300,000 barrels a day, so the 100 million barrels are other Gulf nations’ crude, Saudi, Emirati, Iraqi, heading to market, not Iranian oil seized. Nothing is being taken out of Iran.

It is not a seizure at all. The Pentagon said last week it is “not escorting” anything, only coordinating with ships that want to cross. The New York Times counted about 70 ships over three weeks, well short of the 200 Trump claimed. And his own Energy Secretary contradicted the framing the same day, saying the US is not taking Iranian oil.

The timing seals it. He revealed a classified naval operation the same morning the new inflation number landed at 4.2%, driven by the very war he is fighting, and called the figure great.

The Navy keeping Hormuz open is real, and it matters. “Stealing Iran’s oil every night” is a headline doing a victory lap around an escort mission. Read the operation, not the adjective.

text:
🛰 CrawlHub · Frontline · 12h Brief
Jun 10

US President Trump announced that the US will resume strikes against Iran today, stating that Iran has "taken too long to negotiate a deal" and will "pay the price." This follows a Situation Room meeting to discuss potential large-scale, limited military operations.

🇹🇼🇨🇳 🚨 Taiwan Conducts HIMARS Drills
Taiwan’s military conducted its first-ever live-fire exercise launching US-supplied HIMARS rockets into the Taiwan Strait, directly facing mainland China. Drills took place near a potential Chinese amphibious landing zone, firing multiple test rockets.

🇺🇸🇮🇷 🚨 Trump Threatens Renewed Strikes on Iran
President Trump announced the US will resume "very hard" strikes against Iran today, stating Iran has "taken too long to negotiate a deal" and will "pay the price." He claimed the US has been secretly taking millions of barrels of Iranian oil nightly and seized 22 ships.

🇺🇸🇮🇷 💥 US Strikes Oil Tanker, Casualties Reported
US forces disabled the Palau-flagged M/T Settebello in the Gulf of Oman after its crew refused to comply with orders, firing precision munitions into its engine room. One Indian crew member was killed and two are missing. This marks the eighth vessel disabled in the US naval blockade of Iran.

🇮🇷🇺🇸 🕊️ Qatari Mediation Efforts in Tehran
A Qatari delegation arrived in Tehran for talks on bilateral relations and regional developments, including diplomatic efforts to end the war. This follows consultations with the US to bridge remaining gaps in negotiations.

🇺🇦🇷🇺 💥 Ukrainian Missiles Hit Russian Defense Plant, Oil Refineries
Ukrainian FP-5 Flamingo cruise missiles struck Russia's VNIIR-Progress plant in Cheboksary, which produces navigation modules for missiles and drones. Strikes also hit the Kuibyshev oil refinery and two oil infrastructure sites in the Vladimir region. Satellite imagery confirmed significant damage.

🇮🇱🇱🇧 💥 IDF Strikes Hezbollah Targets in Lebanon
The IDF struck Hezbollah operatives and infrastructure in Tyre and other areas of southern Lebanon, including a weapons cache. Lebanese media reported two dead in an Israeli drone strike in Sidon. Hezbollah claimed an FPV drone hit an Israeli military transport vehicle.

🇷🇺🇺🇦 🚨 Fuel Shortages and Explosions in Crimea
Sevastopol governor Mikhail Razvozhayev reported failed fuel deliveries overnight, forcing tighter restrictions and prioritizing emergency services. Explosions were reported in Oktyabrske and near Cape Fiolent in Sevastopol, with machine-gun fire in Pishchane and Andriivka.

🇺🇦🇩🇪 🛡️ Ukraine and Germany Accelerate Air Defense Ammunition Supply
Ukraine and Germany discussed accelerating the supply of PAC-3 missiles for Patriot systems and ammunition for IRIS-T. They also focused on developing an anti-ballistic project and scaling up drone-assault units.

🇬🇧🇮🇪 🚨 Riots in Belfast Following Attack
Rioters in Belfast confronted police, throwing missiles and setting cars alight, particularly in Newtownabbey. This unrest follows an attempted beheading by a Sudanese migrant, Hadi Alodid, who has been charged with attempted murder.

🇺🇦🚀 🛰️ Ukraine Adapts Neptune Launcher for New Areion Missile-Drone
Ukraine adapted the Neptune missile launcher for the new Areion missile-drone, developed by Luch Design Bureau. Areion is designed for deep strikes against coastal infrastructure with a declared 600 km range and a 100-120 kg warhead.

Evening Briefing — Jun 10

June 10 saw a dramatic escalation in the US-Iran conflict, with the US launching strikes on Iran after blaming Tehran for an Apache helicopter incident near the Strait of Hormuz — an Iranian drone became lodged between two pilots without exploding [1], which Trump called 'a miracle' — and Iran retaliating with missile and drone strikes on US bases in Bahrain, Jordan, and Kuwait, drawing formal condemnations from Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and the GCC. On the diplomatic front, a Qatari delegation traveled to Tehran to bridge remaining gaps in US-Iran nuclear negotiations [2] after Iran refused direct talks, even as Trump repeatedly threatened additional 'hard' strikes, claimed the US has been covertly seizing millions of barrels of Iranian oil nightly [3], and declared US control over the Strait of Hormuz; the IAEA Board of Governors passed a US-backed resolution demanding Iran declare all enriched uranium stocks, opposed by Russia, China, and Iran in a joint statement [4]. Economic fallout continued to mount as OPEC output hit its lowest level since 2000 [5] driven by the US naval blockade and Hormuz disruptions, US inflation surpassed 4.2%, and the US Treasury announced 'Operation Economic Fury' — targeting Iran's global military procurement networks [6] and sanctioning China- and Hong Kong-based entities — while Iranian parliament members issued bellicose warnings that the next war could expand beyond the region and Iran's territorial boundaries.

“Through Economic Fury, the Treasury Department is disrupting the foreign procurement networks that support the Iranian military’s efforts to acquire weapons,” US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement. 

“Treasury has frozen the Iranian regime’s assets, severely disrupted its economy, and dismantled the Iranian war machine.  Treasury will not tolerate any support of the Iranian military,” Bessent added.
